questions.question: What does a perplexity of 1.0 imply about a language model on the test corpus?
options: The model assigns probability 1 to every actual token; it is a perfect model
The model is uniform over the vocabulary
The corpus is empty
The model is broken
questions.explanation: Perplexity equals exp(avg negative log-likelihood). When every token gets probability 1, the NLL is zero, and exp(0) is 1.

questions.question: Why does the harness require non-negative log-probabilities from the adapter?
options: Perplexity is undefined for negative values
Negative log-probabilities are non-negative by definition; receiving a negative value means the adapter sent log p, not -log p
Numpy crashes on negatives
The bin edges in ECE assume positivity
questions.explanation: -log p of a probability is non-negative. A negative value is a contract violation that would silently give perplexity < 1, which is impossible.

questions.question: What does expected calibration error measure?
options: The mean absolute distance between predicted and true labels
The average gap between bin-level confidence and bin-level accuracy, weighted by bin size
The variance of the softmax output
The cross-entropy of the predictions
questions.explanation: ECE bins predictions by confidence, measures the gap between average confidence and average accuracy per bin, weights by bin size.

questions.question: Why does the implementation also return populated_bins alongside the ECE value?
options: It is required for reliability diagrams
It signals when too few bins are populated for the single number to be trustworthy
It is the denominator of the Brier score
It is needed by numpy
questions.explanation: With few populated bins the ECE is dominated by noise. Reporting the populated count lets the runner refuse to publish a misleading single number.

questions.question: What does Brier score capture that ECE does not?
options: It is faster to compute
It penalises local miscalibration directly, since it is the per-prediction squared error
It does not need ground-truth labels
It works only on binary outcomes
questions.explanation: ECE only looks at averages per bin. Brier squares the error per prediction, so it catches the case where bins cancel out on average but are individually miscalibrated.

questions.question: Why is calibration a per-model report rather than a per-task metric?
options: Tasks do not have probabilities
Single tasks have too few predictions to estimate bin statistics; the report aggregates across the whole eval
Calibration is too slow to compute per task
ECE requires the runner to know the model architecture
questions.explanation: Per-task calibration is dominated by noise. The runner pools confidence/correctness pairs across all tasks before computing ECE, Brier, and the reliability diagram.
